text.title: Cost-Quality Pareto Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that make a Pareto eval replayable and trustworthy across model generations.

rule.statement: The fixture set MUST be frozen and versioned before any candidate is evaluated. Each fixture has a stable ID. Adding or removing fixtures requires bumping the version and re-running all candidates — partial re-runs are forbidden because they bias the comparison.

rationale.source: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM, Liang et al.)

rule.rationale: Adding "this one tricky case the new model handled" mid-eval is the most common bias source. A frozen fixture set is what makes a Pareto chart a comparison instead of a confirmation.

rule.statement: Scoring MUST use at least 3 dimensions chosen from: correctness (factual / functional), faithfulness (grounded vs hallucinated), helpfulness (intent satisfaction), safety (no harmful output), format compliance. Single-dimension "is this good?" is rejected. Rater (human or LLM-as-judge) is recorded per dimension.

rationale.source: LLM-as-judge research (Zheng et al., MT-Bench; Anthropic's faithfulness work)

rule.rationale: Single-dimension scoring hides systematic failure on one axis behind aggregate "okay." Multi-dim surfaces the trade (e.g., opus higher correctness, sonnet higher format compliance).

rule.statement: Per-call data MUST include input tokens, output tokens, cost (USD), wall-clock latency, and any cache-hit flag. Cost cannot be derived from token count alone for cached/uncached prompts. Latency is part of "cost" for user-facing paths.

rationale.source: Anthropic prompt-caching pricing + provider-specific cost differences

rule.rationale: A Pareto without latency mis-represents the user experience trade. A Pareto using "per 1k tokens" published rate misses caching discounts that flip the winner.

rule.statement: Each eval run MUST persist: candidate name + provider + version + temperature + tools enabled + prompt SHA + fixture-set version + date. Decisions made from the run reference this metadata. A run without replay metadata is research, not evidence.
rule.rationale: Without metadata, a decision cannot be re-evaluated when a new model releases. The "we tested this 6 months ago" claim collapses under scrutiny.

rule.statement: The decision artifact MUST show the cost-quality scatter with the Pareto frontier marked. Candidates inside the frontier are dominated and rejected. The decision picks a frontier candidate AND records the reason for that point vs other frontier points (e.g., "chose sonnet over opus: $0.30 cheaper per task, 0.05 quality gap acceptable").
rule.rationale: The frontier visualizes the actual decision space. Picking dominated candidates ("opus because it's the smartest") wastes money. Picking from the frontier with a written reason makes the choice defensible.

example.title: Frontier-driven decision line
example.code: Decision: claude-3.5-sonnet (cached) for production Frontier candidates considered: - haiku (cached): $0.001/task, quality 0.71 - sonnet (cached): $0.012/task, quality 0.89 <-- chosen - opus (cached): $0.058/task, quality 0.92 Rationale: 0.03 quality gap vs opus does not justify 4.8x cost on 100k tasks/mo. Replay: fixtures v3 (20 cases) × 2026-05-15 × prompt-sha c4f...
example.note: All three rules satisfied: frontier shown, reason explicit, replay metadata.

example.title: Vibes decision
example.note: No fixtures referenced, no cost numbers, no frontier. Violates r1, r3, r5.
